content.problemStatement:
Let's define the strength of a matrix as the number of distinct rows in it. Two rows a1,a2,‚Ä¶,ana_1, a_2, \dots, a_na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,an‚Äã and b1,b2,‚Ä¶,bnb_1, b_2, \dots, b_nb1‚Äã,b2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,bn‚Äã are distinct if there is an index iii such that ai‚âÝbia_i \neq b_iai‚ÄãÓÄÝ=bi‚Äã.

You are given an integer matrix AAA with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and MMM columns (numbered 111 through MMM). A submatrix of AAA is formed as the intersection of rows r1r_1r1‚Äã through r2r_2r2‚Äã and columns c1c_1c1‚Äã through c2c_2c2‚Äã for any r1‚â§r2,c1‚â§c2r_1 \le r_2, c_1 \le c_2r1‚Äã‚â§r2‚Äã,c1‚Äã‚â§c2‚Äã. Find the sum of the strengths of all submatrices of AAA.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and M ‚Äì the number of rows and columns of the matrix. Then N lines follow, each containing M integers A[i][j] (1 ‚â§ A[i][j] ‚â§ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: A single integer ‚Äì the sum of strengths (the number of distinct rows) of all possible submatrices of the given matrix.

solution.approach:
For a fixed pair of column borders (c1, c2) the submatrix is defined by all rows and the columns between c1 and c2. For every row we can compute a rolling hash of the segment A[row][c1..c2]; the hash uniquely represents the row content inside the current column interval. The problem then reduces to: given an array H[1..N] of hashes, compute the sum over all row intervals of the number of distinct values in the interval. This classic sub‚Äëarray problem can be solved in O(N) using the "last occurrence" trick: iterate rows from top to bottom, keep a map last[hash] = last row index where this hash appeared, and add (i - last[hash]) to the answer (treat last[hash] = 0 when the hash has not appeared yet). Summing this contribution for every column interval yields the required total. The algorithm:
1. If N == 1, answer = M¬∑(M+1)/2 (every submatrix contains exactly one distinct row).
2. For each left column L from 0 to M‚Äë1:
   ‚Ä¢ Initialise an array curHash[0..N‚Äë1] = 0.
   ‚Ä¢ For each right column R from L to M‚Äë1:
        ‚Äì Update curHash[row] = curHash[row] * BASE + A[row][R] (unsigned 64‚Äëbit overflow works as a modulo 2^64 hash).
        ‚Äì Clear an unordered_map lastPos.
        ‚Äì Iterate rows i = 0..N‚Äë1, retrieve last = lastPos[ curHash[i] ] (0 if absent), add (i+1 - last) to the global answer, and set lastPos[ curHash[i] ] = i+1.
3. Output the accumulated answer.
The double loop over columns runs O(M¬≤) times, the inner row scan is O(N). Because N¬∑M ‚â§ 5¬∑10‚Åµ, the worst‚Äëcase work is about (‚àö5¬∑10‚Åµ)¬≥ ‚âà 3.5¬∑10‚Å∏ simple integer operations, which fits easily in the time limit with a fast language.
The rolling hash uses unsigned long long overflow, avoiding explicit modulo operations and guaranteeing good distribution for the given constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M¬≤¬∑N) ‚Äì for each of the O(M¬≤) column intervals we scan all N rows once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) ‚Äì the current hash for each row and the hash‚Äëto‚Äëlast‚Äëposition map.
